title: HSE Management Standards for Work-Related Stress
chapters: HSE Management Standards · Six key areas of work design · Framework for assessing and managing psychosocial risks · Based on MHSWR 1999 reg.3 general risk assessment obligation
desc: The HSE Management Standards for work-related stress define the six key areas of work design that, if not properly managed, are associated with poor health, lower productivity, and increased sickness absence. The six areas are: (1) Demands — workload, work patterns, work environment; (2) Control — how much say the person has in the way they do their work; (3) Support — encouragement, sponsorship, resources from the organisation, line management, and colleagues; (4) Relationships — promoting positive working to avoid conflict, dealing with unacceptable behaviour; (5) Role — whether people understand their role and whether the organisation ensures they do not have conflicting roles; (6) Change — how organisational change is managed and communicated. The Standards are not legally binding — they are a practical framework for implementing the employer's general duty under HSWA 1974 s.2 and the specific risk assessment obligation under MHSWR 1999 reg.3 in relation to psychosocial risks. Stress-related absence is the leading cause of long-term sickness absence in the UK. HSE enforcement activity on psychosocial risks is increasing — improvement notices for stress management failures are now routinely issued.
legalStatus: Non-statutory guidance. The Management Standards are a practical framework, not law. However, the obligation to assess psychosocial risks is part of the general risk assessment duty under MHSWR 1999 reg.3, which is legally binding. HSE has confirmed that stress-related risks must be assessed alongside physical risks (Allison v LUL [2008]).

sections.title: Demands, Control, Support, Relationships, Role, Change
sections.summary: The six Management Standards define the conditions that, if present, reflect a high level of health, well-being and performance. For each standard, the HSE defines: the standard itself (the desirable state), what should be happening (the states to be achieved), and what should not be happening (the states to be avoided). Employers should use the Management Standards as a framework for: identifying the main risk factors for work-related stress, facilitating discussions with employees about the risk factors, developing action plans to address any identified issues, and monitoring and reviewing the effectiveness of actions taken. The process involves five steps: (1) identify the risk factors using the Management Standards Indicator Tool (a validated questionnaire); (2) decide who might be harmed and how; (3) evaluate the risk; (4) record findings and develop and implement an action plan; (5) monitor and review.
sections.text:
The six Management Standards:

1. DEMANDS — Includes workload, work patterns, and the work environment
Standard: Employees indicate that they are able to cope with the demands of their jobs
What should be happening: the organisation provides employees with adequate and achievable demands; people's skills and abilities are matched to the job demands; jobs are designed to be within the capabilities of employees; employees' concerns about their work environment are addressed

2. CONTROL — How much say the person has in the way they do their work
Standard: Employees indicate that they are able to have a say about the way they do their work
What should be happening: where possible, employees have control over their pace of work, are encouraged to use their skills and initiative, and are consulted over their work patterns

3. SUPPORT — Encouragement, sponsorship and resources provided by the organisation, line management and colleagues
Standard: Employees indicate that they receive adequate information and support from their colleagues and superiors
What should be happening: the organisation has policies and procedures to adequately support employees; employees know what support is available and how to access it; employees receive regular and constructive feedback

4. RELATIONSHIPS — Promoting positive working to avoid conflict and dealing with unacceptable behaviour
Standard: Employees indicate that they are not subjected to unacceptable behaviours at work
What should be happening: the organisation promotes positive behaviours at work to avoid conflict; employees share information relevant to their work; the organisation has agreed policies and procedures to prevent or resolve unacceptable behaviour

5. ROLE — Whether people understand their role within the organisation and whether the organisation ensures that they do not have conflicting roles
Standard: Employees indicate that they understand their role and responsibilities
What should be happening: the organisation ensures that the different requirements it places on employees are compatible; employees understand their role and responsibilities; the organisation provides clear information to enable employees to understand their role

6. CHANGE — How organisational change (large or small) is managed and communicated in the organisation
Standard: Employees indicate that the organisation engages them frequently when undergoing an organisational change
What should be happening: the organisation provides employees with timely information to enable them to understand the reasons for proposed changes; the organisation ensures adequate employee consultation on changes and provides opportunities for employees to influence proposals

versions.description: HSE Management Standards for work-related stress first published. Developed following extensive research and consultation.

leadingCases.name: Hatton v Sutherland [2002] EWCA Civ 76
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal set out 16 practical propositions for workplace stress personal injury claims. The key threshold is foreseeability — the employer must have known or ought to have known that the particular employee was at risk. There are no special legal principles for workplace stress claims — they are governed by ordinary negligence principles. Unless the employer knows of a particular vulnerability, they are entitled to assume the employee can withstand the normal pressures of the job.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive authority on the foreseeability threshold for workplace stress personal injury claims — the 16 Hatton propositions

leadingCases.name: Barber v Somerset County Council [2004] UKHL 13
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords approved the Hatton propositions but emphasised that in each case the question is whether the employer's conduct in response to the known risk was reasonable. Once an employer is on notice of a risk to a particular employee's mental health, they must take some positive action. Ignoring warning signs is not an option.
leadingCases.significance: House of Lords authority — once on notice of risk, the employer must act. Approved but nuanced the Hatton propositions.

leadingCases.name: Dickins v O2 plc [2008] EWCA Civ 1144
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that referring an employee to occupational health is not sufficient to discharge the employer's duty if the employer then fails to act on the occupational health recommendations. The employer must take reasonable steps to implement any recommendations — merely making the referral does not satisfy the duty.
leadingCases.significance: Established that an occupational health referral alone is insufficient — the employer must act on the recommendations

refs: HSWA 1974 s.2 (employer's general duty — includes mental health and welfare)
MHSWR 1999 reg.3 (risk assessment — includes psychosocial risks)
HSE Management Standards Indicator Tool (validated stress questionnaire)
Walker v Northumberland County Council [1995] ICR 702 (first successful workplace stress negligence claim)
Allison v London Underground Ltd [2008] ICR 636 (psychosocial risks within scope of reg.3 risk assessment)

pendingAmendments: HSE enforcement on psychosocial risks increasing — improvement notices for stress risk assessment failures
Post-COVID hybrid working — stress risk assessments must cover remote/hybrid arrangements
No ERA 2025 amendments to stress framework directly

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Non-statutory guidance. The Management Standards are a practical framework for implementing the employer's general duty under HSWA 1974 s.2 and the risk assessment obligation under MHSWR 1999 reg.3 in relation to psychosocial risks.
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Not legally binding. However, the obligation to assess psychosocial risks is confirmed by case law (Allison v LUL [2008]). HSE enforcement activity on stress management is increasing — improvement notices for inadequate stress risk assessment are routinely issued.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The Management Standards were developed following extensive HSE research into work-related stress. Stress-related absence is the leading cause of long-term sickness absence in the UK. The Standards provide the practical framework that employers should follow to assess and manage psychosocial risks.

precedingDocuments.title: HSE Research: Stress at Work (1998-2004)
precedingDocuments.significance: The research programme that developed the six Management Standards and the validated Indicator Tool questionnaire.

precedingDocuments.title: Walker v Northumberland County Council [1995] ICR 702
precedingDocuments.significance: The first successful workplace stress negligence claim. Established that the employer owes a duty of care in respect of psychiatric injury caused by work-related stress.

leadingCases.name: Hatton v Sutherland [2002] EWCA Civ 76
leadingCases.heldText: 16 practical propositions for workplace stress claims. Key threshold: foreseeability — employer must have known the particular employee was at risk.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive 16-proposition framework for stress claims.

leadingCases.name: Barber v Somerset CC [2004] UKHL 13
leadingCases.heldText: Once on notice of risk to a particular employee's mental health, the employer must take positive action. Approved but nuanced the Hatton propositions.
leadingCases.significance: House of Lords — duty to act once on notice.

leadingCases.name: Dickins v O2 plc [2008] EWCA Civ 1144
leadingCases.heldText: Referring to occupational health is not enough — employer must act on the recommendations.
leadingCases.significance: OH referral alone is insufficient — must implement recommendations.

leadingCases.name: Allison v London Underground [2008] ICR 636
leadingCases.heldText: Psychosocial risks must be assessed alongside physical risks under MHSWR reg.3.
leadingCases.significance: Stress risk assessment falls within reg.3 scope.
